text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a young patient, conserving both adnexa. The round ligament, fallopian tube, and utero-ovarian ligament are cut one centimeter outside their uterine insertion. The adnexal vein is controlled, and the broad ligament is opened to continue dissection toward the left uterosacral ligament. The posterior lip of the broad ligament is cut to increase the distance between the ureter and the left uterine artery. The same procedure is repeated on the opposite side, with scissors perpendicular to the tissue, allowing easy removal of the adnexa from the uterine attachment. The uterine artery is skeletonized, and the posterior lip of the broad ligament is developed toward the right uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon performs a complex vesicovaginal dissection, carefully cutting small bites of scar tissue under visual control. The assistant retracts the bladder cranially, and methylene blue is used to fill the bladder, allowing the identification of the vesicovaginal plane and checking for potential bladder injury.

The surgeon identifies the Halban fascia to facilitate pulling down the bladder toward the vagina. The medial aspect of the bladder pillar is cut, revealing scar tissue that is coagulated before cutting. Methylene blue is used to fill the bladder, aiding in the safe continuation of the vesicovaginal dissection until reaching the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator. The medial part of the right bladder pillar is cut, and the uterine artery and vein are controlled after coagulation.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy using a monocular needle, cutting the anterior and posterior aspects of the vagina, as well as the left and right lateral aspects and upper parts of the uterosacral ligaments. The bladder remains filled with methylene blue. Hemostasis is achieved using stitches rather than direct coagulation, starting with a stitch inside the uterosacral ligament that includes the vaginal mucosa to reduce postoperative bleeding risk. The vagina is closed with water knots, preferring interrupted sutures over running ones.

The surgeon closes the final knot using an intracorporeal knotting technique with a right wheel hole stitch and a 14mm needle. The vagina is visibly well closed, completing the total laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
